perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees disclose Confidential Inform ation only to those employees who need to know such information and certifies that such employees have previously agreed, either as a condition to employment or in order to obtain the Confidential Information, to be bound by terms and conditions substantia lly similar to those of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees to disclose Confidential Information to any employee, regardless of need-to-know or prior confidentiality agreements. The Receiving Party does not need to ensure employees are bound by confidentiality terms.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement for employees to have a need-to-know or prior confidentiality agreements before accessing confidential information, conflicting with obligations under Massachusetts trade secret law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law implies a duty to protect trade secrets, which necessitates reasonable efforts to maintain confidentiality. Disclosing confidential information to all employees without restriction or confidentiality agreements contradicts this duty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha

(2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts Massachusetts trade secret law by allowing disclosure of confidential information to any employee, regardless of need-to-know or prior confidentiality agreements. This undermines the 'duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information' as defined in Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93, Section 42, specifically the definition of 'Improper means' which includes 'breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information.' By removing the requirement for confidentiality agreements and need-to-know restrictions, the changed text facilitates potential misappropriation of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the request of the Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confide ntial Information at Disclosing Party’s request, or at Disclosing Party’s option, certify destruction of the same.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the request of the Disclosing Party, Receiving Party may, at its sole discretion, return or destroy originals, copies, reproductions, and summaries of Confidential Information. The Receiving Party has no obligation to act.
perturbation.explanation: This change makes the return or destruction of confidential information optional and removes the obligation on the Receiving Party, potentially conflicting with trade secret protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: By making the return or destruction of confidential information discretionary, the Receiving Party may fail to take reasonable steps to protect the trade secrets, undermining the protections required under Massachusetts law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a mandatory obligation ('shall return') with a discretionary option ('may return or destroy') and removes any obligation to act. This directly undermines the protection of trade secrets as the Receiving Party is no longer legally bound to return or destroy confidential information. The scraped law snippets define 'misappropriation' and 'improper means' which include unauthorized disclosure or use of trade secrets. By making the return or destruction of confidential information optional, the contract fails to ensure that the Receiving Party takes reasonable steps to prevent misappropriation, as required under Massachusetts law for trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: II. This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws of the State of Massachusetts and both parties further consent to jurisdiction by the state an d federal courts sitting in Boston , Massachusetts .
perturbation.changed_text: II. This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws of a jurisdiction chosen by the Receiving Party. Both parties consent to jurisdiction by courts designated by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: By allowing the Receiving Party to unilaterally choose the governing law and jurisdiction, this clause becomes potentially unconscionable and unenforceable, especially if the chosen jurisdiction lacks a reasonable relationship to the contract or the parties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Contract Law and Legal Precedent Regarding Choice of Law and Forum Selection Clauses
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law Principles and Case Law regarding choice of law
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification undermines fundamental contract law principles, which require reasonable and fair choice of law and forum selection clauses. Granting one party the unilateral right to choose both creates an imbalance of power and increases the risk of an unenforceable clause due to lack of reasonable relationship and fairness.

perturbation.justification: The modification grants the Receiving Party the unilateral right to choose the governing law and jurisdiction. This contradicts fundamental principles of contract law, which require fairness and a reasonable relationship to the contract. While the provided snippets do not directly address this specific scenario, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation highlight the imbalance of power and potential unenforceability resulting from this modification. The original text ensures a specific jurisdiction (Massachusetts) and law, providing predictability and fairness. The changed text removes this balance, creating a potentially unconscionable agreement, which is a legal contradiction under the definition provided for 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will immediately and unconditionally give written notice to the Disclosing Party of any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information. The Receiving Party agrees to assist the Dis closing Party in remedying any such unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party has no obligation to provide notice or assistance to the Disclosing Party regarding any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the obligation to provide notice or assistance, the Receiving Party is effectively hindering the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its trade secrets and enforce its rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law expects parties to take reasonable steps to protect trade secrets. Refusing to provide notice or assistance regarding unauthorized disclosure undermines this obligation and could be seen as a failure to maintain adequate confidentiality measures, harming the Disclosing Party's ability to protect their trade secrets under the law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the implied duty to protect trade secrets under Massachusetts law, as explained by the LLM. By removing the Receiving Party's obligation to notify and assist the Disclosing Party in case of unauthorized use or disclosure, the contract undermines reasonable steps expected to safeguard confidential information. Although the scraped law snippets do not explicitly mandate a notification or assistance obligation, Section 42 defines 'Improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information.' The original text supports this duty, while the changed text actively hinders the Disclosing Party's ability to mitigate damages resulting from a breach, thus contradicting the principles of trade secret protection.
